narrative: As an Asian male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.10—below the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 31, which is lower than the dataset average of 36.70. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 61.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam, tipping the scales toward rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your academic record that carried the most weight in the decision. Most importantly, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—meaning you placed in the bottom 20% of your peers that year—strongly pushed the model toward forecasting bar failure, as it signals challenges in sustaining strong performance over time. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also in the lower range compared to the average of about 5-6, reinforced this concern by highlighting an early pattern of below-average grades relative to other students. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income provided some counterbalance, nudging the prediction slightly toward success since students from similar backgrounds often have advantages that help with bar passage. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your decile rankings are notably lower, placing you outside the stronger performers the model favors, while your full-time status aligns with the vast majority (over 90%) who succeed. This combination made the model view your bar exam odds as riskier than those of admitted students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study or additional coursework, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience and potential. Many students in similar situations have improved their profiles and succeeded on appeal or in reapplying—your determination as a full-time aspiring lawyer can make the difference. Keep building evidence of your upward trajectory, and explore programs with holistic reviews that value diverse backgrounds like yours.
